articles.title: Solar Tax Credit 2025: Complete Homeowner’s Guide
articles.excerpt: Learn how the 2025 federal solar tax credit works, who qualifies, and how to maximize your savings with solar and batteries.
articles.content:
The 2025 federal solar investment tax credit (ITC) allows eligible homeowners to claim a percentage of their solar installation costs as a credit on their federal income taxes. In 2025, the credit remains at 30% for qualifying solar PV systems and standalone or paired battery storage installed on a primary or secondary residence in the United States.

What costs qualify?
- Solar panels and inverters
- Racking, wiring, and balance-of-system components
- Energy storage batteries of 3 kWh or greater that are charged primarily by solar
- Labor for on-site preparation, installation, and permitting

Key eligibility rules for 2025:
- The system must be placed in service (activated) in the 2025 tax year.
- You must own, not lease, the system to claim the credit directly (leases typically pass savings through via lower payments).
- There is no maximum claim amount, but the credit cannot exceed your tax liability for the year. Unused portions may carry forward depending on your tax situation.

Stacking incentives:
Many states and utilities offer additional rebates and performance incentives that can be combined with the federal ITC. However, some state rebates reduce the net cost basis used to calculate your federal credit. Always consult a tax professional for personalized guidance.

Example:
If your total solar + battery project cost is $24,000 and you qualify for the full 30% credit, your potential federal tax credit is $7,200. Your net cost after the federal credit is effectively $16,800, before any state or utility incentives.

Planning your project:
To capture the 2025 credit, allow enough time for design, permitting, and installation before the end of the year. Our team can provide a detailed proposal that clearly breaks out eligible costs and estimated incentives.

articles.title: Residential Rooftop Solar Basics: What to Expect
articles.excerpt: Understand the key steps in a typical home solar project—from site survey to permission to operate.
articles.content: A residential rooftop solar project typically follows a predictable set of steps: consultation, site survey, system design, permitting, installation, inspection, and utility interconnection. During the site survey, our team evaluates your roof condition, shading, and electrical panel. The design phase optimizes panel layout and system size to match your energy usage and budget. Permitting timelines vary by jurisdiction, usually ranging from 2 to 6 weeks. Installation for an average 6 kW system takes 1–2 days, followed by city inspection and utility approval before your system is turned on.

articles.title: Designing Commercial Solar Carports for Parking Lots
articles.excerpt: Solar carports turn parking lots into energy assets. Learn design basics, clearance rules, and ROI drivers.
articles.content: Solar carports add shade, weather protection, and clean energy production to existing parking lots. Key design considerations include drive aisle widths, clearance heights for delivery vehicles, column spacing, drainage, and lighting integration. For a 50-space lot, typical DC system sizes range from 100 kW to 250 kW depending on bay configuration. Carport structures can be single- or double-cantilever, and can incorporate EV charging stations. Incentives and accelerated depreciation often make carport solar especially attractive for businesses with high daytime loads.

articles.title: How to Size a Home Battery for Backup and Savings
articles.excerpt: Not sure if you need a 10 kWh or 20 kWh battery? This guide walks through backup priorities and bill savings.
articles.content: Battery sizing starts with your goals. For short-duration outage backup of essential loads, a 10–13 kWh battery is often sufficient. For extended outages or whole-home backup, multiple batteries totaling 20–40 kWh may be appropriate. Review your critical loads—refrigeration, lighting, networking, and medical equipment—and estimate their hourly consumption. Pairing batteries with solar maximizes bill savings by storing midday excess for evening use and supporting time-of-use rate arbitrage.

articles.title: Annual Roof and Solar Maintenance Checklist
articles.excerpt: Extend the life of your roof and solar array with a simple annual maintenance routine.
articles.content: A combined roof and solar maintenance plan should include: visual inspection of shingles or membrane, checking for loose flashing, cleaning debris from gutters, thermal scanning for hidden moisture where applicable, visual inspection of solar modules, testing of open-circuit voltage and string current, verification of inverter performance, and cleaning of accessible panels where soiling is significant. Document findings with photos and track issues over time to avoid surprises.

articles.title: What to Do If Your Roof Starts Leaking During a Storm
articles.excerpt: Quick steps to limit damage from an active roof leak before our emergency crew arrives.
articles.content: If you notice an active roof leak during a storm, move valuables away from the leak, place a bucket or container under the drip, and carefully relieve bulging ceiling paint by puncturing a small hole to let water drain in a controlled way. Do not climb on the roof while it is wet or windy. Take photos of the leak and any visible damage for insurance documentation. Our 24/7 emergency team can provide temporary tarping or patching until a permanent repair is scheduled.

articles.title: Solar Loan vs. Solar Lease: Which Is Better Over 20 Years?
articles.excerpt: Compare long-term costs, ownership, and tax benefits of solar loans and leases over a 20-year period.
articles.content: Loans generally provide higher lifetime savings because you own the system and can claim tax credits, but they require good credit and may involve a larger upfront payment. Leases and power purchase agreements (PPAs) shift maintenance responsibilities to the provider and can have low or no upfront cost, but long-term payments may exceed the cost of ownership. When comparing a loan and lease over 20 years, include interest, escalators, maintenance, and tax benefits in your analysis.

articles.title: Draft: Upcoming 2026 Energy Policy Changes
articles.excerpt: Internal draft outlining expected changes to energy policy in 2026.
articles.content: This draft article is used internally to prepare for potential 2026 policy updates. It will be updated and published once details are finalized.
